title: Replace each element with sum of left + sum of right
description: Given an array replace each element with sum of elements on its left and right

examples.explanation: Array: [1,2,3,4], n=4. For i=0: left_sum = sum([])=0, right_sum = sum([2,3,4])=9, result[0]=9. For i=1: left_sum = sum([1])=1, right_sum = sum([3,4])=7, result[1]=8. For i=2: left_sum = sum([1,2])=3, right_sum = sum([4])=4, result[2]=7. For i=3: left_sum = sum([1,2,3])=6, right_sum = sum([])=0, result[3]=6. Final result: [9,8,7,6].
Array: [5], n=1. For i=0: left_sum = sum([])=0, right_sum = sum([])=0, result[0]=0. Final result: [0].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each element, iterate through the entire array to compute the sum of elements to its left and right separately, then replace the element with that sum. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Array Transformation
ladders.desc: Replace each element in an array with the sum of all other elements (left sum + right sum) using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by directly implementing the requirement: for each element at position i, we need to compute the sum of all elements except itself. This is equivalent to calculating the total sum of the array minus the current element. However, the problem explicitly asks for left sum + right sum, which we can compute by scanning the entire array twice for each element: once to sum elements before i (left sum) and once to sum elements after i (right sum). The intuition is straightforward: we treat each element independently and compute its required value by examining all other elements. The algorithm works by: 1) Creating a result array of same size. 2) For each index i from 0 to n-1: a) Initialize left_sum = 0 and right_sum = 0. b) Iterate j from 0 to i-1 to accumulate left_sum. c) Iterate j from i+1 to n-1 to accumulate right_sum. d) Set result[i] = left_sum + right_sum. 3) Return result. Time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n elements, we perform up to n-1 additions (worst-case when i is in the middle). Space complexity is O(n) for the result array, or O(1) if we modify in-place (though careful with overwriting). Connection to L1: This level implements the complete solution by repeatedly calling the core summation logic for each element. Common mistakes include: modifying the original array while reading values (leading to incorrect sums), off-by-one errors in loop bounds, and not handling edge cases like single-element arrays. Real code pattern: def transform_array(arr): n = len(arr); result = [0]*n; for i in range(n): left_sum = sum(arr[:i]); right_sum = sum(arr[i+1:]); result[i] = left_sum + right_sum; return result. Note: Using Python's sum() makes it O(n²) due to slicing overhead.

examples.trace: Array: [1,2,3,4], n=4. For i=0: left_sum = sum([])=0, right_sum = sum([2,3,4])=9, result[0]=9. For i=1: left_sum = sum([1])=1, right_sum = sum([3,4])=7, result[1]=8. For i=2: left_sum = sum([1,2])=3, right_sum = sum([4])=4, result[2]=7. For i=3: left_sum = sum([1,2,3])=6, right_sum = sum([])=0, result[3]=6. Final result: [9,8,7,6].
Array: [5], n=1. For i=0: left_sum = sum([])=0, right_sum = sum([])=0, result[0]=0. Final result: [0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Left and Right Sums for One Element
ladders.desc: Given an array and an index, calculate the sum of elements to the left and right of that index.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. For a specific index i in an array of size n, we need to compute two values: left_sum (sum of elements from index 0 to i-1) and right_sum (sum of elements from i+1 to n-1). The intuition is to isolate the element at i and aggregate all other elements into two groups based on their position relative to i. This is done by scanning the array linearly but skipping the element at i. The algorithm: 1) Initialize left_sum = 0 and right_sum = 0. 2) For j from 0 to i-1: left_sum += arr[j]. 3) For j from i+1 to n-1: right_sum += arr[j]. 4) Return left_sum + right_sum. Time complexity is O(n) for each call, as we traverse up to n-1 elements. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: In the full problem, this sub-routine is called n times (once per element), leading to O(n²) total time. Common mistakes include: including the element at i in the sums, incorrect loop bounds (e.g., using <= instead of <), and not handling cases where i is at the boundaries (left_sum or right_sum should be 0). Real code pattern: def compute_sum_for_index(arr, i): n = len(arr); left_sum = 0; for j in range(0, i): left_sum += arr[j]; right_sum = 0; for j in range(i+1, n): right_sum += arr[j]; return left_sum + right_sum.

examples.trace: Array=[1,2,3,4], i=1, n=4. Compute left_sum: j=0 to 0: left_sum += arr[0]=1 → left_sum=1. Compute right_sum: j=2 to 3: j=2: right_sum += arr[2]=3 → right_sum=3; j=3: right_sum += arr[3]=4 → right_sum=7. Total = 1+7=8.

Array=[5,10,15], i=0, n=3. Compute left_sum: j=0 to -1 (no iteration) → left_sum=0. Compute right_sum: j=1 to 2: j=1: right_sum += arr[1]=10 → right_sum=10; j=2: right_sum += arr[2]=15 → right_sum=25. Total = 0+25=25.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Summation of Array Subsegments
ladders.desc: Calculate the sum of a contiguous subarray given start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental operation of summing elements in a specific range of an array, which is used repeatedly in the brute force approach. Given an array arr, a start index s, and an end index e (inclusive or exclusive), we compute the total of elements from s to e. The intuition is to iterate through the specified indices and accumulate values. This is a building block for computing left_sum (sum from 0 to i-1) and right_sum (sum from i+1 to n-1). The algorithm: 1) Initialize total = 0. 2) For index j from s to e (using appropriate bounds): total += arr[j]. 3) Return total. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of elements in the range (up to n). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: In L1, we call this logic twice: once for the left segment and once for the right segment. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop bounds (e.g., inclusive vs exclusive), not handling empty ranges (where s > e, sum should be 0), and using inefficient methods like slicing in Python (which creates copies). Real code pattern: def sum_subarray(arr, start, end_exclusive): total = 0; for i in range(start, end_exclusive): total += arr[i]; return total. For left_sum: sum_subarray(arr, 0, i); for right_sum: sum_subarray(arr, i+1, len(arr)).

examples.trace: Array=[1,2,3,4], start=0, end_exclusive=2. Iterate i=0 to 1: i=0: total += arr[0]=1 → total=1; i=1: total += arr[1]=2 → total=3. Return 3.
Array=[5,10,15], start=2, end_exclusive=3. Iterate i=2 to 2: i=2: total += arr[2]=15 → total=15. Return 15.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Array Indices
ladders.desc: Loop through an array to access each element or a range of elements.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic skill of traversing an array using loops, which is essential for the summation operations. The intuition is that arrays are indexed collections, and we can use loop variables to access elements sequentially or selectively. This includes iterating over all elements, iterating from a start to an end index, and skipping specific indices. The algorithm involves: 1) Determining the loop bounds (e.g., from 0 to n-1 for full traversal, or from s to e for a subrange). 2) Using a loop variable (e.g., i or j) to access arr[i] within the loop body. 3) Performing operations like accumulation or condition checks. Time complexity depends on the number of iterations (up to O(n)). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: In L2, we use this iteration to sum elements in a subarray by looping through the specified range. Common mistakes include: incorrect loop bounds leading to index out-of-range errors, modifying the array while iterating (if not careful), and using inefficient loop constructs. Real code pattern: For full traversal: for i in range(len(arr)): # access arr[i]. For subrange: for i in range(start, end): # access arr[i]. This builds the foundation for the nested loops in the brute force approach.

examples.trace: Array=[1,2,3], n=3. Loop i=0 to 2: i=0: access arr[0]=1; i=1: access arr[1]=2; i=2: access arr[2]=3.
Array=[5,10,15,20], start=1, end=4 (exclusive). Loop i=1 to 3: i=1: access arr[1]=10; i=2: access arr[2]=15; i=3: access arr[3]=20.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access and Update
ladders.desc: Read a value from an array at a specific index and store a new value at an index.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the most fundamental operations with arrays: accessing an element by its index and updating an element at an index. Arrays are zero-indexed contiguous data structures where each position holds a value. Accessing involves using the index to retrieve the value, e.g., val = arr[i]. Updating involves assigning a new value to that position, e.g., arr[i] = new_val. The intuition is that indices act as addresses, allowing direct lookup and modification. These operations are O(1) time and O(1) space. Connection to L3: In L3, we use access to read elements during iteration, and in higher levels, we update elements to store computed sums. Common mistakes include: using invalid indices (e.g., negative or >= length), confusing zero-indexing with one-indexing, and not considering that updates affect subsequent accesses if not handled carefully. Real code pattern: For access: x = arr[0] # gets first element. For update: arr[1] = 5 # sets second element to 5. This is the atomic action that builds up to more complex array manipulations.

examples.input: Array: [10,20,30], access index 1, update index 2 to 40
examples.trace: Initial array: [10,20,30]. Access index 1: arr[1] = 20. Update index 2: set arr[2] = 40. New array: [10,20,40].

examples.input: Array: [5], access index 0, update index 0 to 0
examples.trace: Initial array: [5]. Access index 0: arr[0] = 5. Update index 0: set arr[0] = 0. New array: [0].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand what an array is and how to use indices to refer to elements.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the basic concept of an array: a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by a numerical index. Arrays are zero-indexed in most programming languages, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The intuition is that an array is like a row of boxes numbered starting from 0; you can look inside a box or put something in it using its number. Key points: 1) Arrays have a fixed size (length) when created. 2) Indices must be within the range 0 to length-1 to avoid errors. 3) Elements can be of any data type (e.g., integers). This foundation is crucial because all higher-level operations (access, update, iteration) rely on understanding indices. Connection to L4: L4 builds on this by performing actual access and update operations. Common mistakes include: thinking arrays start at index 1, using indices beyond the array bounds, and not grasping that indices are integers. Real code pattern: In Python, an array is represented as a list: arr = [1, 2, 3]. Length is len(arr) = 3. Valid indices are 0, 1, 2. This is the starting point for solving array-based problems.

examples.trace: Array has elements: index 0 = 10, index 1 = 20, index 2 = 30. So index 1 refers to the second element, which is 20.

examples.input: Array: [5], what are the valid indices?
examples.trace: Array has length 1. Valid indices range from 0 to 0. So only index 0 is valid, which holds the element 5.

approaches.name: Prefix and Suffix Sums O(n)
approaches.summary: Precompute prefix sums (cumulative sums from the left) and suffix sums (cumulative sums from the right) in two passes. Then, for each element, compute the sum of left and right by combining these precomputed values, and replace the element accordingly.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Replace Elements with Sum of Left and Right Using Prefix/Suffix Sums
ladders.desc: Complete solution: transform array by replacing each element with sum of all elements to its left plus all elements to its right.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires transforming an array where each element becomes the sum of all elements to its left plus all elements to its right. The naive approach would be O(n²) by calculating left and right sums for each element individually. The optimal O(n) approach uses prefix and suffix sums. The intuition is that we can precompute cumulative sums from both directions, then for any position i, the left sum is prefix[i-1] (sum of elements before i) and the right sum is suffix[i+1] (sum of elements after i). By precomputing these in two passes, we avoid redundant calculations. The algorithm: 1) Compute prefix array where prefix[i] = sum of arr[0..i]. 2) Compute suffix array where suffix[i] = sum of arr[i..n-1]. 3) For each i, result[i] = (prefix[i-1] if i>0 else 0) + (suffix[i+1] if i<n-1 else 0). 4) Return result. Time: O(n) for three passes. Space: O(n) for prefix/suffix arrays. Connection to L1: This uses the prefix/suffix computation as a key subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting edge cases (first/last elements), modifying original array while computing, off-by-one errors in indices. Real pattern: compute both prefix and suffix, then combine with boundary checks.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute prefix sums: prefix[0]=1, prefix[1]=3, prefix[2]=6, prefix[3]=10. Step 2: Compute suffix sums: suffix[0]=10, suffix[1]=9, suffix[2]=7, suffix[3]=4. Step 3: For i=0: left sum = 0 (no elements left), right sum = suffix[1]=9 → result[0]=9. i=1: left sum = prefix[0]=1, right sum = suffix[2]=7 → result[1]=8. i=2: left sum = prefix[1]=3, right sum = suffix[3]=4 → result[2]=7. i=3: left sum = prefix[2]=6, right sum = 0 (no elements right) → result[3]=6. Final result: [9, 8, 7, 6].

Step 1: prefix: [5, 15, 30]. Step 2: suffix: [30, 25, 15]. Step 3: i=0: left=0, right=suffix[1]=25 → 25. i=1: left=prefix[0]=5, right=suffix[2]=15 → 20. i=2: left=prefix[1]=15, right=0 → 15. Result: [25, 20, 15].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Prefix and Suffix Sums Arrays
ladders.desc: Precompute cumulative sums from left (prefix) and right (suffix) for an array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the computational engine for the full problem. The intuition: many array problems require knowing cumulative sums up to certain points, and computing them once avoids recalculating. Prefix sums: start from left, each element is sum of all previous elements plus current. Suffix sums: start from right, each element is sum of all following elements plus current. Algorithm: Given array arr of size n. For prefix: initialize prefix[0]=arr[0]; for i from 1 to n-1: prefix[i]=prefix[i-1]+arr[i]. For suffix: initialize suffix[n-1]=arr[n-1]; for i from n-2 down to 0: suffix[i]=suffix[i+1]+arr[i]. Time: O(n) each, total O(n). Space: O(n) for each array. Connection to L0: These arrays provide instant access to left/right sums for any position. Common mistakes: incorrect initialization, wrong loop direction for suffix, off-by-one in indices. Real pattern: simple iterative accumulation with proper starting points.

examples.trace: Prefix: start prefix[0]=1. i=1: prefix[1]=prefix[0]+2=1+2=3. i=2: prefix[2]=prefix[1]+3=3+3=6. i=3: prefix[3]=prefix[2]+4=6+4=10. Suffix: start suffix[3]=4. i=2: suffix[2]=suffix[3]+3=4+3=7. i=1: suffix[1]=suffix[2]+2=7+2=9. i=0: suffix[0]=suffix[1]+1=9+1=10.
Prefix: prefix[0]=5; prefix[1]=5+10=15; prefix[2]=15+15=30. Suffix: suffix[2]=15; suffix[1]=15+10=25; suffix[0]=25+5=30.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Combine Prefix and Suffix Sums for Left+Right Sum
ladders.desc: Given prefix and suffix arrays, compute for each position: sum of elements left + sum of elements right.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight: once we have prefix and suffix sums, we can compute left+right sum for any index i without iterating. The intuition: prefix[i-1] gives sum of all elements before i (left sum), and suffix[i+1] gives sum of all elements after i (right sum). For edges: if i=0, left sum is 0; if i=n-1, right sum is 0. Algorithm: Given prefix, suffix arrays of size n. Initialize result array of size n. For i from 0 to n-1: left = prefix[i-1] if i>0 else 0; right = suffix[i+1] if i<n-1 else 0; result[i] = left + right. Time: O(n). Space: O(n) for result. Connection to L1: Uses the precomputed prefix/suffix from that level. Common mistakes: accessing invalid indices (i-1 when i=0 or i+1 when i=n-1), misunderstanding that prefix[i] includes arr[i] (so we use prefix[i-1] for left sum). Real pattern: simple conditional index access.

examples.trace: For i=0: left=0 (since i=0), right=suffix[1]=9 → 0+9=9. i=1: left=prefix[0]=1, right=suffix[2]=7 → 1+7=8. i=2: left=prefix[1]=3, right=suffix[3]=4 → 3+4=7. i=3: left=prefix[2]=6, right=0 (since i=3=n-1) → 6+0=6. Result: [9, 8, 7, 6].
i=0: left=0, right=suffix[1]=25 → 25. i=1: left=prefix[0]=5, right=suffix[2]=15 → 20. i=2: left=prefix[1]=15, right=0 → 15. Result: [25, 20, 15].

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Cumulative Sum (Prefix Sum) of an Array
ladders.desc: Given an array, compute a new array where each element is the sum of all elements up to that index.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental for many optimization problems. The intuition: instead of repeatedly calculating sums of subarrays, we compute cumulative sums once and then query in O(1) time. Algorithm: Input array arr of size n. Initialize prefix array of size n. Set prefix[0] = arr[0]. For i from 1 to n-1: prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + arr[i]. This builds cumulative sum left to right. Time: O(n). Space: O(n). Connection to L2: Prefix sums are half of what's needed; suffix sums are similar but computed right to left. Common mistakes: forgetting to initialize prefix[0], using wrong loop bounds, misunderstanding that prefix[i] includes arr[i]. Real pattern: iterative accumulation storing previous result.

examples.trace: Start prefix[0]=1. i=1: prefix[1]=prefix[0]+2=1+2=3. i=2: prefix[2]=prefix[1]+3=3+3=6. i=3: prefix[3]=prefix[2]+4=6+4=10.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Through Array and Accumulate Sum
ladders.desc: Traverse an array from start to end, maintaining a running total of elements seen.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is the building block for cumulative computations. The intuition: many algorithms require processing elements sequentially while keeping track of some aggregate value (like sum). Algorithm: Initialize sum = 0. For each element in array: add element to sum. Optionally, store intermediate sums. Time: O(n). Space: O(1) if just final sum, O(n) if storing intermediate values. Connection to L3: This is exactly what prefix sum computation does—it stores each intermediate sum. Common mistakes: not initializing sum to 0, incorrect loop bounds, modifying array while iterating. Real pattern: simple loop with accumulation variable.

examples.trace: Start sum=0. Process element 1: sum=0+1=1. Element 2: sum=1+2=3. Element 3: sum=3+3=6. Element 4: sum=6+4=10. If storing intermediate: [1, 3, 6, 10].
sum=0. Element 5: sum=5. Element 10: sum=15. Element 15: sum=30. Intermediate: [5, 15, 30].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Traversal and Element Access
ladders.desc: Basic skill: loop through an array and read each element's value.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite for any array algorithm. The intuition: arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we access them using indices starting from 0. Algorithm: Given array arr of size n. Use a loop (e.g., for i from 0 to n-1) to visit each element. At each step, you can read arr[i]. Time: O(n) to visit all elements. Space: O(1) if just reading. Connection to L4: This is the first step before adding accumulation—you need to access elements to sum them. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., going to n instead of n-1), confusing index with value. Real pattern: basic for-loop with index variable.

examples.trace: Array indices: 0,1,2,3. i=0: access arr[0]=1. i=1: access arr[1]=2. i=2: access arr[2]=3. i=3: access arr[3]=4.

approaches.name: In-Place with Running Sums O(n)
approaches.summary: Compute the total sum of the array first, then iterate through the array, using a running left sum to calculate the right sum on the fly as total sum minus left sum minus current element, and replace each element with the sum of left and right. This avoids extra arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — In-Place Array Transformation with Running Sums
ladders.desc: Replace each array element with the sum of elements to its left and right using O(1) space.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires transforming an array where each element becomes the sum of all elements to its left and right, excluding itself. The naive approach would use O(n) extra space to store left and right sums. However, we can achieve O(1) space by leveraging mathematical insight: right_sum = total_sum - left_sum - current_element. First, compute the total sum of the array in O(n) time. Then iterate through the array, maintaining a running left_sum that accumulates elements we've already processed. For each element at index i, calculate right_sum = total_sum - left_sum - arr[i]. The new value for arr[i] becomes left_sum + right_sum. Update left_sum by adding the original arr[i] (before modification) to it for the next iteration. This works because total_sum is constant, and left_sum tracks the sum of elements from index 0 to i-1. The key is to store the original arr[i] in a temporary variable before overwriting it, as we need it for updating left_sum. Time complexity is O(n) for two passes (one for total sum, one for transformation), and space complexity is O(1) excluding input storage. This approach is efficient and elegant, avoiding extra arrays while maintaining clarity. Common mistakes include modifying arr[i] before calculating right_sum (which corrupts total_sum logic) or forgetting to update left_sum with the original value. Always handle edge cases like empty arrays or single elements carefully.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute total_sum = 1+2+3+4 = 10. Initialize left_sum = 0. Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=1. right_sum = total_sum - left_sum - arr[0] = 10 - 0 - 1 = 9. new_val = left_sum + right_sum = 0+9=9. Store original = arr[0] = 1. Update arr[0] = 9. Update left_sum += original = 0+1=1. Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=2. right_sum = 10 - 1 - 2 = 7. new_val = 1+7=8. original=2. arr[1]=8. left_sum=1+2=3. Step 4: i=2, arr[2]=3. right_sum=10-3-3=4. new_val=3+4=7. original=3. arr[2]=7. left_sum=3+3=6. Step 5: i=3, arr[3]=4. right_sum=10-6-4=0. new_val=6+0=6. original=4. arr[3]=6. left_sum=6+4=10. Final array: [9,8,7,6] but wait, recheck: For i=0: left_sum=0, right_sum=9, new_val=9. i=1: left_sum=1, right_sum=7, new_val=8. i=2: left_sum=3, right_sum=4, new_val=7. i=3: left_sum=6, right_sum=0, new_val=6. Output [9,8,7,6]. However, problem states sum of left and right elements, not including current. For [1,2,3,4], left of 1 is 0, right sum is 2+3+4=9, total 9. Left of 2 is 1, right sum is 3+4=7, total 8. Left of 3 is 1+2=3, right sum is 4, total 7. Left of 4 is 1+2+3=6, right sum is 0, total 6. Yes correct.

Step 1: total_sum = 5+10+15=30. left_sum=0. Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=5. right_sum=30-0-5=25. new_val=0+25=25. original=5. arr[0]=25. left_sum=0+5=5. Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=10. right_sum=30-5-10=15. new_val=5+15=20. original=10. arr[1]=20. left_sum=5+10=15. Step 4: i=2, arr[2]=15. right_sum=30-15-15=0. new_val=15+0=15. original=15. arr[2]=15. left_sum=15+15=30. Final: [25,20,15]. Verification: For 5, left sum=0, right sum=10+15=25, total 25. For 10, left sum=5, right sum=15, total 20. For 15, left sum=5+10=15, right sum=0, total 15.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Right Sum Using Total and Left Sum
ladders.desc: Calculate right sum for an element given total sum and running left sum.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the O(1) space solution. Given the total sum of the array, a running left sum (sum of elements from start to current index-1), and the current element value, we compute the right sum (sum of elements from current index+1 to end). The formula is: right_sum = total_sum - left_sum - current_element. Intuition: The total sum includes left_sum, current_element, and right_sum. By subtracting left_sum and current_element from total_sum, we isolate right_sum. This avoids needing to compute right sums separately, saving O(n) space. In the algorithm, we iterate through the array, and for each index i, we use this formula to get right_sum instantly. We then compute new value = left_sum + right_sum. After updating the array at i, we increment left_sum by the original current_element (stored before modification) to maintain correctness for the next iteration. Time complexity for this sub-routine per element is O(1), and overall O(n) for the loop. Space is O(1) for variables. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called within the main loop to transform each element. Common mistakes: Using modified array value in formula (must use original), or miscalculating left_sum update. Ensure left_sum starts at 0 and is updated after processing each element.

examples.trace: Given total_sum=10, left_sum=0, current=1. Apply formula: right_sum = total_sum - left_sum - current = 10 - 0 - 1 = 9. This means sum of elements to the right of current is 9. Then new value = left_sum + right_sum = 0+9=9. Update left_sum += current = 0+1=1 for next step.
Given total_sum=30, left_sum=5, current=10. right_sum = 30 - 5 - 10 = 15. New value = 5+15=20. Update left_sum += 10 = 5+10=15.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Mathematical Relation: total = left + current + right
ladders.desc: Understand the equation that enables O(1) space calculation of right sum.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is the mathematical relationship: for any element in an array, the total sum of the array equals the sum of elements to its left, plus the element itself, plus the sum of elements to its right. Formally, total_sum = left_sum(i) + arr[i] + right_sum(i), where left_sum(i) is sum of arr[0] to arr[i-1], and right_sum(i) is sum of arr[i+1] to arr[n-1]. This is derived from the definition of total sum as the sum of all elements. Rearranging gives right_sum(i) = total_sum - left_sum(i) - arr[i]. This identity allows us to compute right_sum without extra storage. Intuition: Since total_sum is fixed, knowing left_sum and the current element lets us deduce right_sum instantly. This transforms an O(n^2) or O(n) space problem into O(n) time and O(1) space. In practice, we precompute total_sum in one pass, then in a second pass, maintain left_sum as we iterate. For each i, apply the formula to get right_sum, compute new value, update array, and increment left_sum by original arr[i]. Time analysis: O(n) for total sum computation and O(n) for transformation, overall O(n). Space: O(1) for variables. Connection to L1: This logic is why the sub-routine works. Common mistakes: Not verifying the equation holds for edge cases (e.g., first/last elements where left_sum or right_sum may be 0). Always test with small arrays.

examples.trace: For array [1,2,3], total_sum = 1+2+3=6. At index 1, element arr[1]=2. left_sum = sum of elements before index 1: arr[0]=1, so left_sum=1. right_sum = sum after index 1: arr[2]=3, so right_sum=3. Check equation: total_sum = left_sum + current + right_sum => 6 = 1+2+3 = 6. Correct. Then right_sum = total_sum - left_sum - current = 6-1-2=3.
Array [4,5,6,7], total_sum=4+5+6+7=22. Index 2: arr[2]=6. left_sum = arr[0]+arr[1]=4+5=9. right_sum = arr[3]=7. Equation: 22 = 9+6+7 = 22. Right_sum = 22-9-6=7.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Total Sum of Array
ladders.desc: Calculate the sum of all elements in an array.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation needed before applying the core logic. The total sum of an array is the sum of all its elements. We compute it by iterating through the array once, accumulating each element into a sum variable initialized to 0. Intuition: Summation is associative and commutative, so order doesn't matter. We need total_sum to use in the formula right_sum = total_sum - left_sum - current. This precomputation takes O(n) time and O(1) space. In the context of the full problem, we compute total_sum in a separate pass before the transformation loop. This ensures total_sum is constant and accurate for all indices. Time analysis: O(n) for a single traversal. Space: O(1) for the sum variable. Connection to L2: Total sum is a key component in the mathematical relation. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle empty arrays (sum should be 0), or integer overflow for large numbers (use appropriate data types). Also, ensure to use the original array values, not modified ones.

examples.trace: Initialize total = 0. Iterate: i=0, arr[0]=1, total = 0+1=1. i=1, arr[1]=2, total=1+2=3. i=2, arr[2]=3, total=3+3=6. i=3, arr[3]=4, total=6+4=10. Final total_sum=10.
total=0. i=0: arr[0]=5, total=0+5=5. i=1: arr[1]=-5, total=5+(-5)=0. i=2: arr[2]=10, total=0+10=10. Final total_sum=10.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Array with Running Accumulator
ladders.desc: Traverse an array while maintaining a running sum of processed elements.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves looping through an array and keeping a cumulative sum of elements seen so far. We initialize a variable (e.g., left_sum) to 0. As we iterate from start to end, for each element, we use the current value of left_sum, then update left_sum by adding the current element. Intuition: This running accumulator allows us to know the sum of all elements up to the current index without recomputing from scratch each time, which would be O(n^2). Instead, it's O(1) per element. In the full problem, left_sum tracks the sum of elements to the left of the current index. After processing an element, we add its original value to left_sum so it's ready for the next iteration. Time complexity for traversal is O(n), space is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used both in computing total sum (where accumulator is total) and in the transformation loop (where accumulator is left_sum). Common mistakes: Updating the accumulator with modified array values instead of original, or incorrect initialization. Always store original value in a temp variable if array is being modified.

examples.trace: Initialize left_sum=0. For i=0: current element=1. Use left_sum=0. Then update left_sum += 1 => left_sum=1. For i=1: current=2. Use left_sum=1. Update left_sum += 2 => left_sum=3. For i=2: current=3. Use left_sum=3. Update left_sum += 3 => left_sum=6. The left sums used at each index are [0,1,3].
left_sum=0. i=0: current=4, use 0, update left_sum=0+4=4. i=1: current=5, use 4, update left_sum=4+5=9. i=2: current=6, use 9, update left_sum=9+6=15. Left sums: [0,4,9].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Traversal and Element Access
ladders.desc: Basic loop through an array to read each element.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding how to iterate over an array. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, accessible by index starting from 0. Traversal means visiting each element exactly once, typically using a for-loop or while-loop. Intuition: We need to access each element to perform operations like summation or modification. In programming, we use an index variable (e.g., i) that goes from 0 to n-1, where n is the array length. For each i, we can read arr[i] and optionally modify it. Time complexity for traversal is O(n), as we do constant work per element. Space complexity is O(1) if we only use a few variables. Connection to L4: Traversal is the mechanism for implementing running accumulators. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (e.g., using i<=n instead of i<n), or not handling empty arrays. Always check array length before looping.

examples.trace: Array has length 3. Initialize i=0. While i<3: access arr[0]=1, print 1. Increment i to 1. i<3: access arr[1]=2, print 2. Increment i to 2. i<3: access arr[2]=3, print 3. Increment i to 3. i<3 is false, stop.
Length=2. i=0: arr[0]=4, print 4. i=1: arr[1]=5, print 5. i=2: stop.
